topic: Bermuda Triangle / East Japan Geometric Symmetry
change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew from 115 to 1078 (+837%) — the change is the addition of a generic post-hoc boilerplate paragraph cross-referencing Part 3.1b. Pre-tldr-schema fingerprint, compared on common fields only. Sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-05T20:52:12.766Z.
our_argument_summary: Verdict remains Refuted by Data — NOAA, Lloyd's, and USCG confirm no anomalous loss rates; the claimed 180° symmetry is actually 152° (short-arc) or 208° (long-arc), 28° off 180° (15.6% deviation); the SAA isn't at Bermuda; agonic lines don't pass through either region. The c3 drift adds a generic post-hoc boilerplate paragraph that creates an internal contradiction (it asserts "the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line" for a WIN our own dome_code_analysis says has no monitor script at all), and cites irrelevant data sources (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records) for what is fundamentally a qualitative geographic-folklore claim.

relevant_files: None — WIN-028 still absent from dome's codebase
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline. raw-text/02-wins.txt has WIN-028 only as a one-line title ("Bermuda Triangle / East Japan Geometric Symmetry CONFIRMED"). raw-text/06-evolution.txt records it added in v50.4 (2026-03-09) with WIN-027. No monitor.py file exists in raw-text/ at all in this snapshot — confirming no code-side validation for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Pure static folklore assertion. No agonic-line position calculation, no insurance-rate retrieval, no geomagnetic comparison, no anomaly-rate ingestion.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The new (c3-drift) post-hoc paragraph in detail_verdict_text asserts "the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line." WIN-028 has no monitor script entry, no data-fetch line, and no hardcoded target. Our own c2 dome_code_analysis explicitly stated this. The boilerplate paragraph thus describes script behavior that does not exist for this WIN — a self-contradiction relative to our own structural claims.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-028 does not appear in any monitor.py domain (no monitor.py in raw-text/ at this snapshot). Site lists it only as a one-line confirmed WIN with no monitoring, validation, or data fetch code. The current code_analysis.monitoring tag in wins.json is "hardcoded" — c2 already flagged this as wrong and recommended changing to "none"; the change has not been applied. Re-flagging because the wrong tag affects Part 2b aggregate counts AND now (combined with hole #1) makes the boilerplate paragraph appear locally consistent on the rendered page when it isn't.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Bermuda Triangle / Devil's Sea "disappearance zone" framing is paranormal folklore, not a standard physics claim being relabeled. Agonic lines are real geophysics but their existence is not what the dome relabels — the dome asserts a novel (and incorrect) folklore-to-geomagnetism correlation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Bermuda Triangle legend: Gaddis 1964, Berlitz 1974. Devil's Sea legend: Berlitz 1974. Agonic line positions: mapped since the 17th century (Halley 1701). Adopted in dome v50.4 (2026-03-09) per raw-text/06-evolution.txt. Pure retrodiction of decades- to centuries-old material.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site connects disc_radius=20015 km, firmament_height=9086 km, sun_altitude=5733 km, or moon_altitude=2534 km to the longitudes of Bermuda or Devil's Sea. The "180° symmetry" is asserted, not derived. (Quantitative check below shows the actual longitude separation is 152° / 208°, not 180°.)

holes_found.description: Internal contradiction introduced by the c3 post-hoc boilerplate. The new detail_verdict_text paragraph asserts: "the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line." Our own c2 review's dome_code_analysis explicitly states WIN-028 is completely absent from the dome's codebase — no monitor script, no data-fetch line, no hardcoded target. A dome defender reading the WIN page can compare this verdict text to our own structural-tag display (when monitoring="none" is correctly set) and call out: "Your debunk says my code does X; your tag display says my code does nothing. Pick one." Worse, monitoring is currently mis-tagged as "hardcoded" (see next hole), which masks the contradiction in the rendered page even though it remains in the underlying argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace the generic post-hoc paragraph with a WIN-028-specific one that doesn't describe code behavior (e.g., note that the legend pre-dates the dome by 50+ years and the longitude positions were established centuries before the dome model existed; no monitoring code is required to identify post-hoc adoption of public folklore), or (b) gate the boilerplate insertion on monitoring∈{hardcoded, live_fetch} so monitoring=none WINs get a different post-hoc framing focused purely on temporal-priority of the underlying observation.

holes_found.description: code_analysis.monitoring tag still wrong after c2 explicitly recommended fix. c2 (2026-04-07) flagged that the tag should be "none" not "hardcoded" because WIN-028 has no monitoring code at all. Current value (verified 2026-05-05): still "hardcoded". Tag persists incorrectly through the c3 verdict-text rewrite. This affects Part 2b aggregate counts and (combined with hole #1) creates a worse problem: the wrong tag may make the boilerplate paragraph look consistent on the page when it isn't.
holes_found.recommendation: Set wins.json[id=028].code_analysis.monitoring = "none". Re-run any Part 2b count-rendering after the change. If the data flows through {{CA_HARDCODED}} / {{CA_NONE}} placeholders, that should auto-correct.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN consistency: 16 WINs with monitoring=none (001, 013, 022, 033, 040, 041, 042, 043, 048, 052, 054, 059, 060, 063, 066, 067) contain the identical post-hoc boilerplate "the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line." For each of these the boilerplate is structurally false in the same way it is for WIN-028 — there is no monitor script to hardcode targets in. WIN-001 has been customized (Tesla 1899 notes / 1905 patent) so the data-source list reads naturally; the others use a generic geomagnetic-flavored list (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records) that is incongruous for non-geomagnetic claims (e.g., WIN-048 CMB Axis of Evil; WIN-052 RAR lensing; WIN-054 El Gordo cluster; WIN-066 NH heat asymmetry; WIN-067 Antarctic gravity hole). This is the kind of cross-WIN pattern a dome defender or AI adversary will surface as "templated boilerplate, not analysis."
holes_found.recommendation: Open an issue (or a section-level review) covering all 26 WINs with this paragraph (16 monitoring=none, 8 hardcoded, 2 live_fetch). For monitoring=none WINs, replace the script-behavior sentence with one focused on temporal priority of the underlying observation. For the geomagnetic-flavored data-source list, either move it to Part 3.1b as a class-level paragraph and have each WIN cite Part 3.1b, or actually customize the data sources per WIN. The current state — same template inserted everywhere with mismatched data sources — undermines our "no boilerplate, item-by-item" rhetorical posture.

holes_found.description: 208° vs 180° quantitative correction (c2 moderate) still not added to detail_evidence. The current text only says "the dome's claimed 180° symmetry does not correspond to any pattern in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models" — abstract and hand-wavable. The concrete arithmetic (Bermuda ~25°N 71°W, Devil's Sea ~25°N 137°E, longitude separation 152° short-way / 208° long-way, 28° off 180°, 15.6% deviation) is a falsifiable rebuttal a defender cannot dismiss. Verified independently in c3 (see quantitative_verification).
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence a sentence such as: "The Bermuda region (~25°N, 71°W) and Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) are separated by 152° of longitude on the short arc (208° on the long arc) — 28° off 180°, a 15.6% deviation that exceeds the model's own tolerance thresholds elsewhere."

holes_found.description: Post-hoc test framework is a poor fit for a qualitative folklore claim. Part 3.1b's post-hoc/prospective framework is built around numerical predictions checked against measured values ("the confirming value"). WIN-028's dome claim is qualitative — geographic symmetry between two folklore zones — there is no "confirming value" to pre-register. The boilerplate paragraph imports the numerical-prediction vocabulary ("the confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset") for a claim that isn't numerical. A dome defender can correctly note we're applying a numerical-prediction stress test to a non-numerical claim.
holes_found.recommendation: For qualitative claims like WIN-028, the post-hoc argument should be framed temporally without the value-vocabulary: "The Bermuda-Triangle and Devil's-Sea legends were published in the 1960s-1970s; agonic-line positions have been mapped since the 17th century. None of this material was generated after the dome model — all of it was available decades before the prediction was registered. The post-hoc carve-out at Part 3.1b requires the data to be temporally posterior to the prediction, which it isn't."

holes_found.description: Generic NOAA homepage link still in detail_evidence (c2 flagged, not fixed). Defender could note we're citing noaa.gov rather than NOAA's specific Bermuda Triangle page.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace https://www.noaa.gov/ with https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/facts/bermudatri.html and quote the relevant NOAA statement.

holes_found.description: Agonic-line positions relative to Bermuda and Devil's Sea still not stated. The c2 review noted that the western agonic (~80°W) only grazes the Triangle's edge and the eastern agonic (~100-120°E) lies well west of the Devil's Sea at 137°E. Including this strengthens the rebuttal because the dome's framing depends on the agonic lines actually passing through the regions.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: "Neither agonic line actually passes through its associated region — the western agonic line at ~80°W only grazes the Triangle's western edge, and the eastern agonic at ~100-120°E lies 17-37° west of the Devil's Sea (137°E)."

stronger_arguments.description: Agonic lines migrate westward over decades due to secular variation of the geodynamo. The western agonic line passed through the Bermuda area in the 1700s-1800s and has since moved well west. A static dome cavity cannot explain time-varying agonic positions — the very pattern the dome claims is geometric is in fact dynamic and timestamp-dependent. Positive evidence AGAINST a fixed dome geometry.
sources: Mandea & Korte (2011), Geomagnetic Observations and Models, Springer

stronger_arguments.description: If the dome's 180° claim were taken seriously, you could pick any pair of folklore zones approximately half a world apart and call it a "symmetry." The dome offers no falsifier — what longitude separation would have been considered NOT 180°? The c2 review correctly noted this is a 15.6% deviation; per the dome's own tolerance thresholds elsewhere (e.g., WIN-029 cited 5.3% as a confirming match), 15.6% should fail by the model's internal standards.
sources: data/wins.json:WIN-029 detail_claim (5.3% threshold cited as confirmation)

kernel_of_truth.description: Agonic lines are real and historically important geomagnetic features (data/wins.json:028 cites them implicitly via the "two locations" framing). Their use in 18th-century navigation is well documented. The dome correctly identifies that there are roughly two primary agonic loci on Earth's surface (raw-text/02-wins.txt:WIN-028).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing that agonic lines exist (https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/facts/bermudatri.html notes Earth's magnetic features in passing) does not justify (i) attaching them to the Bermuda/Devil's-Sea folklore which has zero evidence base per NOAA/Lloyd's/USCG (data/wins.json:028 detail_evidence), (ii) treating their separation as 180° when it is 152°/208° (quantitative_verification below), or (iii) ignoring that they migrate over decades, which a static dome (config: disc_radius=20015 km, firmament_height=9086 km) cannot explain.

deeper_analysis:
The c3 drift on WIN-028 is concentrated in detail_verdict_text — a generic post-hoc boilerplate was inserted that nominally strengthens our review (adds a second independent ground for rejection: post-hoc adoption) but in practice introduces two new vulnerabilities. First, the boilerplate describes nonexistent monitor-script behavior for a WIN our own structural analysis says has no monitor script. Second, the boilerplate's data-source list (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records) is incongruous for what is fundamentally a folklore-symmetry claim. Combined with the still-mis-tagged monitoring=hardcoded value, the c3 state is in some ways weaker than c2 — the verdict is still correct but the supporting argument is now self-contradicting in a way that an attentive dome defender or AI adversary will exploit.

The cross-WIN scope of this issue is what elevates the third hole to major. The boilerplate appears in 26 WINs (16 with monitoring=none). For monitoring=none WINs the structural claim is uniformly false. This is the kind of pattern that turns one local finding into a class-level finding — a single 'templated debunk paragraph' that a defender can demonstrate by quoting two or three of our own WIN pages side-by-side. We should fix this site-wide, not just on WIN-028.

The verdict (Refuted by Data) is solidly correct. The dome's underlying empirical claim has no factual basis (NOAA, Lloyd's, USCG), the geometric-symmetry sub-claim is quantitatively wrong (152°/208° not 180°), and the only reason a more aggressive verdict like 'Self-Contradicted' doesn't apply is that there is no internal-to-the-model contradiction here — the claim is just empirically and geometrically false. The recommended action remains minor_edit (single paragraph rewrite + monitoring tag fix), not verdict_change.

Note on the 208° vs 152° framing: c2 used '208° separation' as the descriptor; the short-arc separation is actually 152°. Both yield the same 28° / 15.6% deviation from 180°. Our text should disclose both arcs to be airtight ('152° short-arc, 208° long-arc, 28° off 180° either way').

Other-side check (per operator preference): the strongest reading FOR the dome here is that 'symmetry' was meant loosely — two folklore zones on roughly opposite sides of the world. Under that loose reading, 152° is more than a quadrant and qualifies as 'roughly opposite.' Our preempt for this is to (a) quote the dome's exact wording so the strawman charge fails and (b) point to the dome's own elsewhere-tight tolerances (e.g., WIN-029 5.3%) to show 15.6% is not 'roughly' by their own standards. This is sufficient if both moves land in the text; the current text does neither.

citations_verified: Bermuda longitude ~71°W, Devil's Sea longitude ~137°E (well-known geographic fact)
Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the Bermuda region (longstanding public position)
USCG: no unusual causes for Bermuda losses (longstanding public statement)
Bermuda Triangle legend popularized Gaddis 1964 / Berlitz 1974 (standard reference)
citations_failed: https://www.noaa.gov/ — generic homepage link still used (c2 flagged, c3 still unfixed). Specific page exists at https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/facts/bermudatri.html.
citations_unchecked: IGRF model — no specific DOI cited, just model name
CHAOS-7 model — no specific DOI cited, just model name
Mandea & Korte 2011 (Springer) — referenced in c2 stronger_arguments but not in the page itself

advocate_mode.best_defense: Imagine I am the dome author reading my own WIN-028 page on this critical-review site. I see the verdict text now contains a generic paragraph that asserts: 'the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line.' But WIN-028 has no monitor script — it never did, and the critical reviewers have themselves admitted as much in their own structural-tag analysis. I quote-screenshot the contradiction and post: 'The reviewers debunk a script that doesn't exist. They also assert the regions are not 180° apart, but the dome's claim was never that they are exactly antipodal — only that two loci of geomagnetic interest line up roughly opposite each other in folklore. The reviewers compute 152° short-arc themselves, which is more than half the planet — i.e., on opposite sides — exactly what the WIN claims. They have manufactured a strawman of mathematical antipodality and are now critiquing themselves.' Then I add: 'The same paragraph appears verbatim across 25 of their other WIN pages with the same data-source list (IGRF, NASA stations, tide gauges, telluric records) regardless of whether the WIN involves any of those sources. They aren't reviewing — they're find-replacing.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Two text changes preempt nearly all of this: (1) gate the post-hoc boilerplate on monitoring∈{hardcoded, live_fetch} OR rewrite it to drop the script-behavior sentence; (2) explicitly quote the dome's exact '180° symmetry' wording from the source page before computing 152°/208° — that anchors the strawman charge. The find-replace charge is harder to dismiss; it requires the cross-WIN fix (hole #3) — addressing it on WIN-028 alone leaves 25 other pages exposed.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-028 shares the c3-drift post-hoc boilerplate with 25 other WINs. For 16 of those WINs (monitoring=none) the boilerplate's structural-tag claim — that the monitor script hardcodes targets on the data-fetch commit — is false. WIN-001 has been customized (Tesla 1899 / patent 1905) so the same paragraph reads naturally; WIN-028 (folklore), WIN-048 (CMB), WIN-052 (RAR lensing), WIN-054 (El Gordo), WIN-066/067 (Antarctic/heat asymmetry) all carry the geomagnetic-flavored data-source list (IGRF, NASA stations, tide gauges, telluric records) regardless of whether those sources have anything to do with the WIN. The pattern is detectable by anyone diff-ing two WIN pages. Cross-WIN consistency fails.

claims_checked: Longitude separation between Bermuda Triangle (~71°W) and Devil's Sea (~137°E)
quantitative_verification.computations: Bermuda ~25°N, 71°W. Devil's Sea ~25°N, 137°E. Eastward separation = (137 - (-71)) mod 360 = 208°. Westward / short-arc separation = 360 - 208 = 152°. min(208, 152) = 152° (the short geodesic arc on a parallel). Deviation from 180°: |180 - 152| = 28° (or |208 - 180| = 28° going the other way). Percentage deviation: 28/180 = 15.555…% ≈ 15.6%. This matches and refines the c2 review's '208° separation, 28° off 180°, 15.6% deviation' framing. The c2 phrasing was correct on the deviation but used the long-arc number; the short-arc is 152°.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: None in our claims. The c2 '208°' framing is correct on its own terms (long-arc), but disclosing both arcs (152° short-arc, 208° long-arc, 28° off 180° either way) is more rigorous and forecloses a dome-defender retort that we picked the unflattering arc.
